title: Sinking Feelings

jokes.text: Narrator sets up the cactus as a breakup signal — 'I got a cactus, for God's sakes. That's a universal sign for things are over.' — while the actual message was 'I ain't going nowhere.'
jokes.setup: Narrator explains Michael was being 'less than honest' about breaking up with Rebel; Michael gave her a cactus claiming it's a universal breakup symbol.
jokes.punchline: Cut to what he actually told her: 'I ain't going nowhere.' — the cactus means the opposite of what he claimed.
jokes.explanation: The cactus as a fabricated 'universal symbol' is a perfectly Michael Bluth move — self-serving logic delivered with confidence. The immediate cut to the contradicting truth is crisp Arrested Development structure. Rewatch bonus because the cactus recurs as a plot device.

jokes.text: Both Michael and George Michael independently claim to have plans with Rebel, each lying to the other, with the narrator revealing both are lying.
jokes.setup: George Michael says he has plans with Rebel; Michael says 'Good for you guys.' George Michael says 'I do, yeah. It's fun.'
jokes.punchline: Narrator reveals the truth for both: George Michael hasn't seen her since punching Michael; and each fears the other will call them out for lying.
jokes.explanation: The parallel lying structure is elegant — both characters are doing the same thing for the same reason, and the narrator peeling back the layers simultaneously is quintessential AD construction. The 'He's gonna say, And that's worse' line is a perfect comic button on the self-awareness of their mutual deception.

jokes.text: G.O.B. instructs someone (likely a surrogate) to carry a 'bag of lacy ticklers, hoo-ha bras, dog toys, and sex knobs' and act like it's a booty call, referring to it as what 'old people think the young people say.'
jokes.setup: G.O.B. is coaching someone on how to approach Rebel's apartment with a bag of sex toys.
jokes.punchline: 'As the old people think the young people say' — G.O.B.'s patronizing generational confusion and the absurd item list ('dog toys') undercut any confidence.
jokes.explanation: Only G.O.B. would frame a booty call as a coaching session with a 'Mr. Brash' persona and slip 'dog toys' into a list of sex accessories without comment. The generational condescension ('old people think the young people say') is a sharp character joke — G.O.B. is always performing an idea of cool he doesn't actually have.

jokes.text: Michael, seeing his son approach, 'decided to be the adult in the relationship and avoid conflict by squatting in a bush.'
jokes.setup: Narrator frames Michael's decision to hide as 'being the adult in the relationship.'
jokes.punchline: The adult move is: crouching in a bush to avoid his son.
jokes.explanation: This is a near-perfect example of the AD narrator's technique: framing the most childish behavior in the most grown-up language. 'Decided to be the adult in the relationship' followed immediately by 'squatting in a bush' is a precision comic reversal. The economy is exceptional — the joke lands in one sentence with zero waste. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: George Michael, bag of sex toys in hand, yells outside Rebel's door: 'Not getting any less horny out here. How are you?'
jokes.setup: George Michael is performing the 'booty call' persona G.O.B. coached him on, standing outside Rebel's apartment with sex toys.
jokes.punchline: 'Not getting any less horny out here. How are you?' — the abrupt politeness at the end utterly deflates the attempted caddishness.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. George Michael trying to be G.O.B.-level brash and immediately collapsing into 'How are you?' is a perfect character moment — his essential niceness can't be suppressed. The contrast between 'not getting any less horny' (trying to be cool) and 'How are you?' (his actual self) is sharp, earned, and deeply character-specific. Highly quotable. Justifies 8.6 craft score.

jokes.text: Michael is discovered squatting in the bushes by a cop — the cop says 'Oh, this one, too?' when Michael explains there's a woman obsessed with him.
jokes.setup: Michael is hiding in a bush outside Rebel's apartment. A cop finds him and Michael starts explaining — 'There's a woman in there who's obsessed with me.'
jokes.punchline: Cop: 'Oh, this one, too?' — implying he's seen this situation many times at this same address.
jokes.explanation: The cop's world-weary 'this one, too?' is a great quick joke — implying Rebel has multiple men hiding in her bushes is funnier than anything the scene directly says. Economy is exceptional but character integration is low (any cop could say this).

jokes.text: Michael asks about Buster — 'Did anybody claim him?' — as if Buster is lost property. Cop confirms: 'They got him. I had a feeling.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if his brother Buster (reported as a missing person) showed up.
jokes.punchline: 'Did anybody claim him?' treats Buster as an unclaimed object; the cop's 'They got him. I had a feeling' treats this as routine.
jokes.explanation: 'Did anybody claim him?' is a darkly funny dehumanization of Buster, consistent with how the Bluth family treats him as less than a full adult. The casual delivery and the cop's total indifference compound the joke well.

jokes.text: Michael sees where the cop lives — 'I live here' — and responds, 'Oh. Didn't know they paid you that fair.' Then: 'Yeah, good for you.'
jokes.setup: The cop reveals he lives in Rebel's building — an expensive apartment.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'Didn't know they paid you that fair' is awkward class-commentary; 'Yeah, good for you' is a deflating afterthought.
jokes.explanation: Mild joke about cop salaries delivered with Michael's characteristic condescension. Functional but not especially sharp or memorable.

jokes.text: George Michael sees Michael emerging from the bushes talking to a cop and says flatly: 'That is one dirty cop. Dad.'
jokes.setup: George Michael watches his father emerge from bushes with a cop — could look like a bribe or improper meeting.
jokes.punchline: 'That is one dirty cop' — meaning the cop literally has dirt on him from the bushes — followed by the recognition 'Dad.'
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed two-beat joke. 'Dirty cop' sets up an idiomatic reading (corrupt) which is immediately recontextualized as literal (covered in bush dirt), then capped with the mortified 'Dad.' Three comic elements in six words — exceptional economy. The pause between 'cop' and 'Dad' is the whole joke.

jokes.text: George Michael offers to 'run up' Michael's breakup message to Rebel to 'save you a trip,' with the condition 'Make sure she doesn't think you're trying to break up.'
jokes.setup: Michael needs to tell Rebel they're broken up. George Michael offers to deliver the message.
jokes.punchline: He undercuts it immediately: 'Make sure she doesn't think you're trying to break up' — he's sabotaging his own father's breakup message for self-interested reasons.
jokes.explanation: The competitive father-son dynamic over the same woman produces this quietly sinister offer. George Michael performing helpfulness while actively undermining his father is solid character comedy.

jokes.text: The scuba exchange: 'You reconsidered the scuba trip? Oh, God, it's tempting as hell... You're in love. So in love. Maybe next time? Promise? I do.'
jokes.setup: Michael is about to embark on a scuba trip. They say goodbye with unexpected emotional intimacy about Michael being 'in love.'
jokes.punchline: The exchange mimics wedding vows — 'I do' — which is absurdly formal for a goodbye about a scuba trip, and also points to Michael's romantic confusion.
jokes.explanation: The vow-like 'I do' at the end of a scuba goodbye is a nice absurdist touch but it plays more as a sweet moment than a hard laugh. Competently executed.

jokes.text: Rebel's ex/lover arrives at her door and George Michael improvises: 'Thought I'd get lucky. Well, you'd get lucky.'
jokes.setup: The visitor asks how George Michael found out Rebel was back.
jokes.punchline: George Michael starts with the clichéd 'get lucky' phrasing, then self-corrects to flip the subject — catching himself being presumptuous and awkwardly redirecting.
jokes.explanation: Solid quick joke on George Michael's inability to maintain a suave persona — his social awkwardness breaks through even in a casual line. Functional.

jokes.text: The delivery man tip exchange: Rebel's guest says 'I hope he's not expecting a tip.' Delivery man: 'They instruct us not to ask.' George Michael: 'I don't have any cash.' Narration: 'George Michael decided to split the difference. Just take it. Jerk. Kidding. Sorry. Thank you. Beat it.'
jokes.setup: An awkward moment about tipping a delivery man with a bag of sex toys.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's attempt to 'split the difference' between generosity and the other man's dismissiveness results in a rapid-fire stream of contradictory instructions: 'Just take it. Jerk. Kidding. Sorry. Thank you. Beat it.'
jokes.explanation: The escalating verbal collapse — 'Just take it. Jerk. Kidding. Sorry. Thank you. Beat it.' — is a perfect encapsulation of George Michael trying to be tough and immediately apologizing, then being rude again. The rhythm of the six-word sequence is the joke's mechanism, and it's precise. Justifies 8.0 craft score.

jokes.text: 'Tough guy. Thinks he's a tough guy. But he does a nice job. As do these sex toys I caddishly brought you.'
jokes.setup: George Michael tries to recover from his fumbled tip exchange by dismissing the delivery man.
jokes.punchline: 'As do these sex toys I caddishly brought you' — he narrates his own behavior using the word 'caddishly,' which no actual cad would say.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character joke. 'Caddishly' is the perfect word for George Michael — he's narrating the role G.O.B. gave him in real-time because he can't actually inhabit it. No actual cad describes himself as caddish. The self-awareness-without-self-correction is peak George Michael Bluth. Highly quotable. Justifies 8.2 craft score.

jokes.text: Rebel discovers the Shoedini in the sex toy bag — 'What is this used for?' Michael explains it's for putting shoes on without bending over. Then: 'Get Shoedini right this second!' and 'I can think of another use for it.'
jokes.setup: A bag of 'sex toys' contains what turns out to be an As-Seen-On-TV shoehorn on a stick (the Shoedini), which Rebel mistakes for something sexual.
jokes.punchline: The explanation is completely mundane ('without bending over!'), then Rebel's 'I can think of another use for it' re-sexualizes it after the deflation.
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection — the 'sex toy' is an infomercial product. The double pivot (sexual → mundane → sexual again) is well-structured. 'It's a shoehorn on a stick!' delivered as revelation is funny in itself. The Shoedini is a real product, which adds absurdist texture.

jokes.text: Rebel says 'I've gotta be gone in five minutes. It's gonna be a quickie.' — which was the exact line George Michael had planned to use, so he improvises: 'Fine, but I hope we can spend some quality time together later.'
jokes.setup: G.O.B. had coached George Michael to say 'I'm here for a quickie and I'm out of here.' Rebel beats him to it.
jokes.punchline: George Michael, outmaneuvered, switches to his natural mode — the thoughtful, quality-time-seeking romantic — which earns him 'Oh, my God, you're the dream boyfriend.'
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed callback payoff. G.O.B.'s entire coaching scheme is undone because Rebel says the line first, and George Michael's authentic self — the opposite of what G.O.B. wanted — accidentally produces the best possible outcome. The irony that being himself works better than the plan is perfect character comedy. Justifies 8.3.

jokes.text: 'Oh, my God, you're the dream boyfriend. Well...'
jokes.setup: George Michael has accidentally done everything right — being sweet, considerate, bringing gifts (even weird ones).
jokes.punchline: Rebel calls him the 'dream boyfriend' and his response is 'Well...' — because he's literally been trying to do the opposite of that all scene.
jokes.explanation: The 'Well...' is the punch. George Michael has tried to be a cad all episode and succeeded as a romantic — his deflated, confused reaction beat is the final payoff of the whole sequence.

jokes.text: The Arrested Development theme song plays as the title card appears over an awkward freeze frame, then: 'It's...'
jokes.setup: The show's title sequence begins after a particularly absurd sequence.
jokes.punchline: The title card moment ('It's...') is a self-aware beat — the show commenting on its own title card placement mid-absurdity.
jokes.explanation: A structural joke about the show's own format. The title card interruption has diminishing returns across the series but still works as a meta beat for longtime fans.

jokes.text: Michael 'handed his son a breakup cactus' — the narrator retroactively reframes the cactus as a breakup gift to George Michael.
jokes.setup: Earlier Michael claimed the cactus was a breakup signal for Rebel. Now the narrator says he gave it to his son.
jokes.punchline: The cactus meant nothing as a symbol — he's just passing it off to George Michael as a breakup gift now, revealing its arbitrariness.
jokes.explanation: The cactus callback is efficient and dark — Michael can't even commit to the lie about what the cactus means; he just recycles it. The narrator's deadpan 'breakup cactus' framing makes it land.

jokes.text: The Bluths are 'graciously receiving the Family of the Year award they were generously giving themselves at the banana stand.'
jokes.setup: The Bluth family is about to receive an award.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals they invented and are awarding themselves the 'Family of the Year' award — and it's at their own banana stand.
jokes.explanation: The Bluths creating an award to give themselves is perfect character comedy — no family better represents this kind of narcissistic self-congratulation. 'Generously giving themselves' is a great narrator construction.

jokes.text: Maeby's plan: have someone show up with meth teeth saying 'Why do I gotta ask my cousin for meth money, Mom?' to embarrass Lindsay on camera.
jokes.setup: Maeby is running a scheme to sabotage Lindsay's political campaign speech with a planted actor.
jokes.punchline: The plan involves rotted-out meth teeth and the specific accusation 'why do I gotta ask my cousin for meth money' — the absurd specificity of the embarrassment.
jokes.explanation: Maeby's schemes are always darkly elaborate, and 'meth teeth' as a prop plus the precise accusatory line is wonderfully specific and nasty. The 'yeah, we're circling it' response suggests the scheme is still being workshopped, adding to the absurdism.

jokes.text: 'Where are my meth teeth? It's been over two hours!'
jokes.setup: Maeby is waiting for her sabotage props to arrive.
jokes.punchline: The complaint 'Where are my meth teeth? It's been over two hours!' treats prosthetic meth teeth as a normal production item with normal delivery expectations.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy. The complaint is so matter-of-fact about the absurdity of the item being complained about. 'Where are my meth teeth?' is a perfect non sequitur-as-normalcy joke — one of the episode's most quotable lines.

jokes.text: Tobias in a costume 'had finally found a way to slip into the family' — revealed to be dressed as a banana stand worker (or similar) for the ceremony.
jokes.setup: Narrator says Tobias 'had finally found a way to slip into the family.'
jokes.punchline: The 'way' is a costume — Tobias is once again using performance to insert himself into a situation he doesn't belong in.
jokes.explanation: A reliable Tobias character joke — he's always 'getting into' things through costumes and performance. 'Slip into' has a double meaning (literally putting on a costume) that's standard AD wordplay. Competent but not among the episode's best.

jokes.text: G.O.B.'s MC work: 'This is O.C. Can You Say by the Sea. We're bringing you the first annual Family of the... Of the Year! Let's hear it for the... of the Year!'
jokes.setup: G.O.B. is MCing the Family of the Year ceremony.
jokes.punchline: He can't even complete the name of the award he invented — 'Family of the... of the Year!' — and his attempt at enthusiasm ('Let's hear it for the...') trails into the same gap.
jokes.explanation: G.O.B.'s incompetence as an MC — failing at the most basic function of announcing the award — is well-executed character comedy. His grandiose confidence paired with total failure is his defining trait.

jokes.text: 'I thought there was gonna be music or... Thank you, Beard, for...'
jokes.setup: G.O.B.'s ceremony is not going as planned — no music, sparse crowd.
jokes.punchline: He thanks someone named 'Beard' — either a literal person or his word for someone in the crowd — as a desperate attempt to pad the failing ceremony.
jokes.explanation: The 'Beard' reference is either a recurring character nod or pure absurdism. The awkward silence beat of the ceremony failing works as cringe comedy. Functional.

jokes.text: George Sr. reads a letter from G.O.B. about their Mexico trip, which includes: 'always horny shirts,' 'eating chips in the car,' 'Shut up, G.O.B.!' and 'almost making love to some roadside bushes.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. reads a touching letter about memories with his father, written by G.O.B.
jokes.punchline: The 'memories' include 'almost making love to some roadside bushes' — a callback to Michael hiding in bushes earlier this episode, but written by G.O.B. as a genuine treasured memory.
jokes.explanation: This is a multi-layered callback: 'almost making love to some roadside bushes' references Michael in the bushes earlier AND is consistent with G.O.B.'s own bizarre history. The list structure (shirts, chips, 'Shut up G.O.B.!', bushes) escalates to the perfect absurd climax. That 'Shut up, G.O.B.!' is listed as a happy memory is its own dark joke about George Sr.'s parenting. One of the episode's best moments. Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: 'Remember how our Winnebago got demolished by that train? So many memories.'
jokes.setup: The letter lists fond memories of the Mexico trip.
jokes.punchline: A Winnebago getting demolished by a train is listed as a fond memory — 'So many memories' follows as if the train destruction was the highlight.
jokes.explanation: Treating catastrophe as nostalgia is a solid dark comedy beat. 'So many memories' after 'train demolished our Winnebago' is a good button. Slightly undercut by following the stronger 'bushes' joke.

jokes.text: 'You are the golden anchor of this family, the man who not only left his mark on Newport with the great banana... stand... up. Good habit. Standing is... Yep. You always leave your mark everywhere you sit.'
jokes.setup: G.O.B.'s letter describes George Sr. as leaving his mark on Newport 'with the great banana stand.'
jokes.punchline: George Sr. reads '...banana... stand... up.' and improvises 'Good habit. Standing is... Yep. You always leave your mark everywhere you sit' to cover the letter's awkward phrasing.
jokes.explanation: The wordplay pivot from 'banana stand' to 'stand up' to 'standing is good' to 'you leave your mark everywhere you sit' is a cascade of quick pivots that shows George Sr. improvising in real time. The double meaning of 'leave your mark' (literal/scatological) is classic AD layering.

jokes.text: 'So I give you the biggest anchor of gold in the world. I only wish it were real gold so that you wouldn't have 30 years of hard work ahead of you.' 'Thirty years! What a horrible speech.'
jokes.setup: The letter presents a golden anchor award to George Sr., who is holding it.
jokes.punchline: 'I only wish it were real gold so that you wouldn't have 30 years of hard work ahead of you' — the 'wish' is that the fake gold award were real so he wouldn't have to work, which is both an insult and illogical. 'Thirty years!' and 'What a horrible speech' are the button.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's 'What a horrible speech' is the best part — the whole elaborate letter being dismissed in three words by the person who should be most moved is great. The '30 years' line is G.O.B.'s inadvertent cruelty.

jokes.text: George Sr. holds the anchor award: 'Let 'em try to take this away from me, huh?' Response: 'They'll have to pry it out of your cold, dead hands.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is defiant about keeping his self-awarded prize.
jokes.punchline: 'They'll have to pry it out of your cold, dead hands' — a standard idiom, but delivered to a man who has faked his death and evaded law enforcement, adding dark weight.
jokes.explanation: The idiom has extra charge given the Bluth family's history with fake deaths, but the joke doesn't fully exploit that resonance. Moderately funny as delivered.

jokes.text: Lindsay arrives 'in what was her second outfit of the day.' Lucille: 'You're running to fill a House seat, not a sofa.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay arrives at the ceremony in an outfit Lucille finds inappropriate for a political campaign.
jokes.punchline: 'You're running to fill a House seat, not a sofa' — the outfit is so large/casual it's compared to sofa upholstery.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best one-liners. 'House seat, not a sofa' is a double meaning (Congress/furniture), perfectly economical, and completely Lucille — her contempt for Lindsay weaponized as fashion criticism with a political pun. The line structure is excellent: setup with 'running to fill a House seat' then the pivot to 'sofa.' Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: 'I'm starting to worry you're not even capable of saying something nice about me in your speech.' 'Lindsay, saying nice things about you is easy. The hard part is not sounding sarcastic.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay worries Lucille can't say anything nice about her.
jokes.punchline: Lucille assures her it's easy — but the hard part is not sounding sarcastic. The compliment and the backhanded insult are inseparable.
jokes.explanation: A perfect Lucille line. The construction is immaculate: she appears to defend herself ('saying nice things about you is easy'), then immediately reveals the insult underneath ('not sounding sarcastic'). This could only come from Lucille — it's a maternal put-down that's technically a compliment and actually a devastating commentary on their relationship. Justifies 8.3.

jokes.text: 'That outfit shouldn't even come in that size.'
jokes.setup: Lucille continues criticizing Lindsay's outfit.
jokes.punchline: 'That outfit shouldn't even come in that size' — not an insult about Lindsay's body directly, but about the fashion industry for producing it.
jokes.explanation: The joke is exceptional because of its redirection: Lucille doesn't say 'you're too fat for that outfit' — she blames the manufacturer for making it available, which is somehow more cutting. The indirection makes it crueler and funnier. Perfectly economic at eight words. Justifies 8.0.

jokes.text: Lindsay wraps herself in a sheet in protest. 'You don't like anything under this. Why should I take it off?' 'You don't have a speech for me.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay responds to Lucille's outfit criticisms by putting on a sheet.
jokes.punchline: 'You don't like anything under this. Why should I take it off?' — a logical but absurd escalation of the fashion argument; Lindsay's defiance is wearing a literal sheet in public.
jokes.explanation: The visual of Lindsay in a sheet at a political event is a solid escalation. 'You don't like anything under this' is good defiant logic. The 'You don't have a speech for me' pivot is a nice non sequitur that reveals Lindsay is more wounded by the speech than the outfit.

jokes.text: 'Look who grew out of her sheet.' — Lucille's comeback when Lindsay returns without the sheet.
jokes.setup: Lindsay takes the sheet off after Lucille says 'The cameras are getting this.'
jokes.punchline: 'Look who grew out of her sheet' — a pun on 'grew out of her shell' that also insults Lindsay's size, all in five words.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best lines. 'Grew out of her sheet' is a triple play: it's a pun on 'grew out of her shell,' a size insult (she grew OUT of it), and a dismissal of her protest. Five words doing the work of twenty. The wordplay is genuinely elegant and completely character-specific to Lucille. Justifies 8.7.

jokes.text: The scuba instructor: 'Know what your number one tool is for a scuba diver? Your sun.' Meanwhile Michael is having a breakdown about leaving his son.
jokes.setup: Michael is on a boat heading out to sea. A scuba instructor is explaining tools. Michael is worried about leaving his son.
jokes.punchline: 'Your sun' — homophones: 'your sun' (the celestial body) and 'your son' (Michael's child), the latter being exactly what Michael is thinking about. The narrator then confirms Michael's fear about 'leaving behind his son.'
jokes.explanation: A classic AD homophone joke — 'your sun/your son' — placed at the exact moment Michael is consumed with guilt about his son. The structural coincidence is too perfect to be accidental, and the show knows it. The narrator's immediate follow-up 'leaving behind his son' confirms the double meaning is intentional. Well-earned and satisfying. Justifies 8.1.

jokes.text: Michael and George Michael shout increasingly emotional declarations at each other from the boat and dock as it moves farther away — 'I don't want to leave things emotionally ambiguous!' / 'I don't want to leave without having some sense of the value of this relationship!'
jokes.setup: Michael's boat is pulling away. He tries to go back. George Michael swam out to say something. Both are yelling.
jokes.punchline: The more distance grows, the more earnestly therapeutic their shouted declarations become — 'emotionally ambiguous,' 'value of this relationship' — until they can no longer hear each other at all.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the gap between the emotional sincerity of the declarations and the absurd logistics of shouting them across growing water. 'Value of this relationship' is therapist-speak that becomes funnier the louder and more desperate it's delivered. Character-specific for both Bluths.

jokes.text: 'And without the ability to speak, they resorted to a language they'd learned long ago.' — Michael and George Michael communicate via hand signals learned in a previous scene/season.
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael can no longer hear each other across the water.
jokes.punchline: They communicate using hand signals — a callback to a previously established shared language, which resolves their emotional standoff.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's 'a language they'd learned long ago' is warmly ironic — it's both sweet and absurd that they have a hand signal system. The visual gag of two people doing sign language across water to resolve an emotional crisis is quintessential AD. High rewatch value for fans who recognize the callback.

jokes.text: George Michael explains he jumped in because he dropped his prize and was trying to find it. 'I would've been fine.' / 'Maybe I was trying to save you.'
jokes.setup: Michael rescues George Michael from the water, assuming he was drowning or suicidal. 'Were you trying to kill yourself?'
jokes.punchline: 'No. I dropped my prize, and I was trying to find it.' Michael's heroic rescue was for a dropped object. Then George Michael adds: 'Maybe I was trying to save you.'
jokes.explanation: The deflation of the dramatic rescue (Michael saves George Michael from... trying to retrieve an object) is classic AD anti-climax. 'Maybe I was trying to save you' flips the dynamic and adds a layer of competitive emotional manipulation.

jokes.text: 'Want to save me? Stop playing games with me. Stop trying to teach me a lesson by hiding Buster.' / 'We would never make you worry. We love you. That's why we got rid of your $700,000 debt.'
jokes.setup: Michael accuses Lucille of staging Buster's disappearance to teach him a lesson. Lucille denies it.
jokes.punchline: 'We would never make you worry. We love you. That's why we got rid of your $700,000 debt' — the declaration of love is immediately followed by a financial maneuver that upends Michael's life.
jokes.explanation: The Bluth family's twisted expression of love — eliminating someone's debt without telling them — is perfectly character-consistent. The pivot from 'we love you' to 'that's why we got rid of your $700,000 debt' is a beautifully timed revelation.

jokes.text: 'Well, no, technically, we forgot the debt. We can forget it, but we can never forgive it. We need the write-off.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if they forgave the debt. Lucille clarifies.
jokes.punchline: 'We can forget it, but we can never forgive it' — a subversion of the idiom 'forgive and forget,' revealing that the Bluths' version is purely transactional: forgetting (as an accounting move) is fine; forgiving (the emotional act) is unthinkable.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. 'We can forget it, but we can never forgive it' is a perfect inversion of the idiom that simultaneously reveals Lucille's character (forgiveness is impossible, forgetting is an accounting decision), the Bluth family's ethos (everything is transactional), and the absurdity of their logic. It's also the most quotable line in the episode. The follow-up 'We need the write-off' is the perfect button — the reason they 'forgot' the debt is tax strategy. This is professional-grade comedy writing at its best. Justifies 9.1.

jokes.text: Michael's theory about the fake Buster: 'I declared Buster missing, and then they were gonna try to teach me a lesson, right? By having the fake Buster over at Lucille 2's today when I walked in.' Response: 'How did they know you were gonna walk in today?' / 'It was a last-minute trip. You just came down to get your scuba gear.'
jokes.setup: Michael lays out his elaborate theory about a conspiracy to fool him with a fake Buster.
jokes.punchline: The theory collapses instantly when Lucille points out they couldn't have known he was coming — 'It was a last-minute trip.' The narrator: 'It wasn't a half bad point.'
jokes.explanation: Michael's elaborate conspiracy theory deflating because of a simple logistical oversight is classic AD — Michael's self-importance leads him to over-construct explanations. 'It wasn't a half bad point' is a perfectly dry narrator line. The joke is also structurally interesting because the logic-hole is the punchline.

jokes.text: Lucille's speech: 'I want to make special mention of my only daughter, who thinks I didn't write a speech.' / Lindsay: 'She stole my speech.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay commissioned Maeby to write a speech for Lucille to give about Lindsay. Lucille is now giving what appears to be a genuinely moving speech.
jokes.punchline: The speech begins: 'who thinks I didn't write a speech' — Lucille is aware of the scheme; and Lindsay immediately recognizes: 'She stole my speech.'
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Lucille co-opted the speech and turned it against both its creators is a layered twist. 'She stole my speech' is the perfect reaction — Lindsay was trying to manipulate her mother and got outmaneuvered. The callback structure rewards attention.

jokes.text: 'She's got a daughter who has many faces, and all of them are beautiful.' / Lindsay: 'She stole my speech.' / Lucille: 'Maybe you can show the world the smile I love so much.' / Maeby: 'That doesn't make sense without meth teeth!'
jokes.setup: Lucille delivers Maeby's speech as her own; the speech was designed around the meth teeth gag that never materialized.
jokes.punchline: 'That doesn't make sense without meth teeth!' — Maeby's scheme required the prop that never arrived, so the speech is meaningless without it.
jokes.explanation: A perfect payoff to the meth teeth running gag. The props never arrived, the speech was stolen, and the scheme is ruined — 'That doesn't make sense without meth teeth!' is both a logical complaint and a completely absurd sentence to say at a political event. The specificity of Maeby's outrage is the joke.

jokes.text: 'What a waste of a dream.' / 'It's nothing. I can see that now.' — Lindsay and Maeby after the speech genuinely moves Lindsay.
jokes.setup: Lucille's stolen speech genuinely moves Lindsay, who wanted to hear those words. Maeby, who wrote the speech to humiliate her mother, watches this unfold.
jokes.punchline: 'What a waste of a dream' — Maeby dismisses Lindsay's moment of genuine emotion as a waste because it didn't produce the desired humiliation.
jokes.explanation: Maeby's contempt for her mother's genuine emotional moment is darkly funny — the scheme failed and the collateral damage is that Lindsay got what she actually needed. 'What a waste of a dream' is quotable and specifically Maeby.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I think I'm gonna go now. Find out who my real family is. Find my real mother.' Narrator: '...Lindsay stole a line from Michael.' / Maeby: 'I'm out of here.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is leaving the ceremony to find her 'real family,' echoing a line Michael often uses.
jokes.punchline: The narrator explicitly notes that 'Lindsay stole a line from Michael' — her declaration of leaving is so characteristically Michael that even the show calls it out.
jokes.explanation: The narrator as explicit joke-tagger ('Lindsay stole a line from Michael') is a meta move that works because it's both self-aware and character-revealing. The Bluth family all use the same dramatic exit line. Then Maeby immediately does the same thing, deepening the joke.

jokes.text: Lindsay decides to 'find her real family' and 'find her real mother' — stealing Michael's exit line
jokes.setup: Lindsay has a dramatic realization and announces she's leaving to find her real family
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes '...Lindsay stole a line from Michael' — the dramatic exit line 'I'm out of here' is recycled Bluth melodrama
jokes.explanation: The narrator's observation that Lindsay is stealing Michael's exit line is a meta-joke about Bluth dramatic exit speeches being a family tradition/crutch. The callback enriches it for long-time viewers. Good use of the narrator as ironic commentator.

jokes.text: George Michael tries to kiss Maeby for the cameras to get a reaction, but Maeby says 'That takes the fun out of it.' They consider kissing again 'to teach them a lesson.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby (cousins) have a history of almost-kissing as a running gag. George Michael wants to do it for the cameras.
jokes.punchline: Maeby's 'That takes the fun out of it' — the fun of the almost-kiss was always the transgression; doing it for cameras is too performative. Then: 'Maybe we should kiss again to teach them a lesson.'
jokes.explanation: The show's long-running cousin-kissing gag being acknowledged and subverted ('that takes the fun out of it') is a smart meta move. Maeby's logic — the transgression only works if it's not sanctioned — is perversely coherent.

jokes.text: Cops arrive: 'You're presenting awards on a crime scene, and that is, of course, against the law.' G.O.B.: 'Why? Because the banana stand is gone?' Cop: 'No, because of the bloody trail that led to it. Ah, I've said too much.'
jokes.setup: Police arrive to shut down the ceremony. G.O.B. assumes it's about the missing banana stand.
jokes.punchline: 'No, because of the bloody trail that led to it. Ah, I've said too much.' — the cop reveals a bloody trail then immediately regrets the disclosure.
jokes.explanation: 'Ah, I've said too much' is a perfect comedy beat — a cop accidentally revealing crucial information and immediately trying to walk it back. The escalation from 'missing banana stand' to 'bloody trail' in one exchange is expertly paced. The dark implication (Lucille 2's fate) lands as both funny and ominous.

jokes.text: Tobias in his backup Buster costume: 'And my name is Buster Bluth, and, yes, I am a monster.' / 'Buster? Isn't he supposed to be in jail?' / 'Well, that's not exactly what I was going for, but sure.' / 'Oh! But they're buying it, see?'
jokes.setup: Tobias has put on a Buster costume as a backup plan. He announces himself as Buster.
jokes.punchline: The family's first response is 'Buster? Isn't he supposed to be in jail?' — which Tobias takes as a WIN ('they're buying it!') even though they've just contradicted the impersonation's premise.
jokes.explanation: Tobias believing the family 'bought' his Buster impression when they've literally just said Buster should be in jail is a perfect Tobias delusion. 'That's not exactly what I was going for, but sure' is a great self-aware concession before the unjustified victory lap. Justifies 8.1.

jokes.text: 'I wanted to make it clear that I'm never coming back again.' Michael immediately after: 'Okay, I will stay.'
jokes.setup: Michael told Buster to go to the police to make a point about leaving. The narrator introduces his new decision.
jokes.punchline: 'And that's when Michael decided to try out a new line: Okay, I will stay.' — Michael's entire 'I'm leaving forever' arc collapses into 'I will stay' the moment he has information.
jokes.explanation: The running gag of Michael threatening to leave and immediately reversing gets a nice structural button here: 'tried out a new line' implies he's aware he has a line he always uses and this is an experiment. The self-awareness makes the callback land better than usual.

jokes.text: 'That would wipe me out, Mom.' Lucille: 'This is your fault. You lied to us about Buster being missing to teach us a lesson.' Michael: 'Because you lied to Buster that I agreed to look after him.'
jokes.setup: The family argues about who is responsible for Buster being in jail and who should pay his bail.
jokes.punchline: The argument reveals nested lies: Michael lied about Buster being missing, because Lucille lied to Buster about Michael's agreement — a Matryoshka doll of Bluth deceptions.
jokes.explanation: The layered lying structure is very AD — everyone is lying to teach someone else a lesson about their lying. The comedic content is less in any specific line and more in the absurdist logic of the whole system.

jokes.text: 'For the last time, I was not there. Or, if I was there, I have no memory.'
jokes.setup: Michael says he saw Lucille at the pier the night Buster disappeared and asked her to look after him.
jokes.punchline: 'For the last time, I was not there. Or, if I was there, I have no memory.' — the qualifier 'Or, if I was there, I have no memory' completely undermines the categorical denial that preceded it.
jokes.explanation: A perfect Lucille line. 'For the last time, I was not there' is a confident categorical denial — then 'Or, if I was there, I have no memory' is both plausible deniability AND a complete self-contradiction delivered without any awareness of the contradiction. This is Lucille's entire character in one sentence: confident lying that casually demolishes itself. Justifies 8.4.

jokes.text: Only one family member is allowed in to see Buster. 'You're the one he's gonna want to see.' / 'He didn't want to see me last time I talked to him. He said he was all grown up and found a new mother.' / 'What a baby thing to say.'
jokes.setup: Buster apparently rejected Lucille as his mother, declaring himself 'grown up.'
jokes.punchline: Lucille's response to Buster saying he's 'all grown up' is 'What a baby thing to say' — perfectly reversing the claim with deadpan contempt.
jokes.explanation: 'What a baby thing to say' in response to someone declaring they're grown up is one of the sharpest and most perfectly Lucille lines in the episode. It's a paradox that makes complete sense — asserting maturity is itself immature — delivered with total contempt for Buster's independence claim. Five words. Near-perfect economy and character integration. Close to the 8.0 anchor quality.

jokes.text: 'I think this is a job for the man in charge... of the president, so, Dad.'
jokes.setup: Michael suggests someone go in to see Buster. He nominates 'the man in charge.'
jokes.punchline: 'The man in charge... of the president' — a jab at George Sr. being controlled by Lucille while nominally being in charge.
jokes.explanation: The joke is clean — 'man in charge... of the president' subverts the idiom to reveal George Sr.'s subordinate position. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: George Sr. plans his approach to the prison guard: 'I don't think it's gonna help if I go in and take a swing at a guard. Uh, maybe I stab one of them.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is about to go into the prison to talk to Buster. He muses about how to handle the guards.
jokes.punchline: 'Maybe I stab one of them' — offered casually as an alternative to punching a guard, as if escalating from assault to stabbing is a reasonable progression of options.
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s casual consideration of prison violence is darkly funny, especially since he's a man who has spent time in prison himself. The escalation from 'punch' to 'stab' delivered deadpan is a solid character beat.

jokes.text: 'No, the only way you're stabbing anything is if you have a knife.' / 'That's what he meant.' / 'That's what I meant.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells George Sr. the only way he'd be stabbing is if he had a knife — as a rhetorical dismissal.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. confirms: 'That's what he meant' — he DOES have a knife. Michael meant it as impossibility; George Sr. hears it as confirmation.
jokes.explanation: A perfect misdirection joke. Michael's rhetorical conditional ('only if you have a knife') is taken literally, and George Sr.'s 'That's what he meant / That's what I meant' — both agreeing — is the punchline. The shared confirmation between father and son that stabbing is on the table is darkly absurd. Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I can smell her on you.' / Michael: 'Ah. Right. I forgot, you're good at that.' / The story about Buster being lost in Nordstrom's because Lucille changed her perfume.
jokes.setup: Buster knows Lucille was outside the prison because he can smell her perfume on Michael.
jokes.punchline: 'I forgot, you're good at that' — acknowledging Buster's ability to track his mother by scent as a normal trait. The backstory: he was lost in Nordstrom's when she changed perfumes; found in the break room with the Chanel man.
jokes.explanation: The Buster-Lucille codependency expressed through scent-tracking is deeply disturbing and therefore deeply funny. 'I forgot, you're good at that' treats a son being able to track his mother by smell as a skill rather than a pathology. The Nordstrom story — found in the break room with the Chanel man — adds an absurdist layer with its own internal story. Among the episode's most character-specific jokes. Justifies 8.6.

jokes.text: 'He should never have lost his job for that. What happened? He was just giving me a Fanta! I know I should've spoken up for him, but I was only a boy. Barely out of my teens. I didn't...' 'Currently.'
jokes.setup: Buster defends the Chanel man who was found with him in the Nordstrom break room.
jokes.punchline: 'I know I should've spoken up for him, but I was only a boy. Barely out of my teens. I didn't...' — Michael interrupts: 'Currently.' Buster is currently barely out of his teens emotionally.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's two best jokes. The structure is immaculate: Buster explains he couldn't advocate for the man because 'I was only a boy, barely out of my teens, I didn't...' — and Michael's single word 'Currently' lands as both an interruption and a complete character assessment. Buster IS still basically a child. The economy is flawless — one word is the entire punchline. This is comparable to the calibration anchors' level of craft. Justifies 9.0.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Yeah. That never happened.' Buster: 'It was at Nordstrom's...' Michael: 'Yes. And we found you in the break room with the Chanel man.'
jokes.setup: Buster recounts being lost in a park; Michael corrects him
jokes.punchline: Michael's correction places Buster not in a park but in a Nordstrom's break room with a Chanel fragrance salesman — somehow worse and more specific
jokes.explanation: Michael's correction is excellent — 'Yes. And we found you in the break room with the Chanel man' implies something far more troubling than getting lost, and the specificity ('Chanel man' vs. 'Charlie girl') adds an additional layer. The fact that Michael confirms it, matter-of-factly, as if this is completely normal, is the core of the joke. Very high character integration for both Michael and Buster.

jokes.text: Buster explains he was supposed to 'lay low' by going to the station and saying he wasn't missing. 'That's what I did. I was supposed to be mother bait, but mother never came to pick me up.'
jokes.setup: Michael told Buster to go to the police station and say he wasn't missing — as a maneuver.
jokes.punchline: 'I was supposed to be mother bait, but mother never came to pick me up.' — Buster describes himself as bait to trap his own mother, then notes she didn't show up, which he registers as a maternal rejection.
jokes.explanation: 'Mother bait' is a perfect Buster phrase — he can only understand his own instrumentalization in terms of his mother's attention. The fact that she didn't come to 'pick him up' (like a child from school) is the dark core of the Buster-Lucille dynamic crystallized in one line. Justifies 8.1.

jokes.text: Buster told police Lucille 2 was 'dead.' 'Maybe they said dead and I said I wish.' / 'Why'd you do that?' / 'I thought it was funny.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks why Buster told police Lucille 2 was dead rather than just passed out.
jokes.punchline: 'I thought it was funny' — Buster's explanation for accidentally incriminating himself in a potential murder is that he was attempting humor.
jokes.explanation: 'I thought it was funny' as the explanation for accidentally confessing to murder is a perfect Buster line. The dark comedy of the character's social obliviousness — not understanding why joking about someone being dead to police is problematic — is sharp.

jokes.text: Buster tried to erase the security tape using Fakeblock — his nephew's fake software. 'Unfortunately, to do so, he used his nephew's software, Fakeblock, which was fake, so it couldn't block anything. But it was easy to dance to.'
jokes.setup: Buster tried to erase security footage. He used George Michael's app.
jokes.punchline: Fakeblock is fake — it can't block anything — but 'it was easy to dance to,' the narrator adds, which is both an absurd consolation and a callback to the Fakeblock running gag across the season.
jokes.explanation: 'But it was easy to dance to' is a perfect throwaway addition — a non-sequitur consolation that somehow fits perfectly. The Fakeblock callback rewards longtime viewers, and the 'easy to dance to' absurdist button makes it land for everyone.

jokes.text: The evidence guy: 'Thanks. You can go.' / 'You had me come all the way down here so I could play a video off our Dropbox?' / 'Okay, evidence guy, we're done with you.'
jokes.setup: The evidence was accessed via Dropbox. The evidence technician who walked a long way is dismissed.
jokes.punchline: The evidence guy's outrage about his unnecessary journey ('Walking a mile to my parking spot 'cause you can't admit you don't know how to use Dropbox!') versus the detective's dismissal.
jokes.explanation: An observational workplace joke about tech incompetence. Functional, relatable, but not particularly sharp or character-specific. The 'walking a mile to my parking spot' detail adds texture. Competent.

jokes.text: The evidence guy: 'He's as much of a detective as someone with detective in their title.'
jokes.setup: The detective dismisses the evidence guy's complaint.
jokes.punchline: 'He's as much of a detective as someone with detective in their title' — a tautology that sounds like an insult but confirms the person IS a detective.
jokes.explanation: The tautological insult — 'as much of a detective as someone with detective in their title' — is a small gem of logic-comedy. It sounds disparaging but is literally a confirmation. The evidence guy apparently doesn't realize he's paid someone a compliment.

jokes.text: Buster's hand was metal-strapped because 'people were using it to make tattoos when I was asleep.' The other strap: 'This is so I don't take the strap off.'
jokes.setup: Buster tries to make air quotes but can't because of his metal restraints.
jokes.punchline: He explains: people were tattooing themselves with his hook hand while he slept, so they strapped it. The second strap prevents him from removing the first strap.
jokes.explanation: The hook hand being used as a tattoo tool on sleeping Buster is a wonderfully specific prison absurdism, and the recursive logic of the second strap preventing removal of the first is perfectly Buster. His complete acceptance of this arrangement is the joke — it doesn't occur to him to resist. Justifies 8.1.

jokes.text: Buster waived his rights to an attorney. 'What for?' / 'You told me to.' / 'Plus, I didn't want the evidence guy to have walked all that way for nothing.'
jokes.setup: Michael discovers Buster waived his legal rights without understanding why.
jokes.punchline: 'I didn't want the evidence guy to have walked all that way for nothing' — Buster sacrificed his legal rights out of consideration for the evidence technician's commute.
jokes.explanation: An extraordinary callback and character joke. Buster waived his legal rights — not out of ignorance, not out of cowardice, but out of CONSIDERATION for the evidence guy's parking situation. This is Buster's bottomless, misplaced empathy crystallized perfectly. The callback to the evidence guy's complaint is immediate and devastating in its absurdism. One of the episode's standout moments. Justifies 8.8.

jokes.text: Barry is in the prison shower: 'And the inmates hate me, 'cause what is a defense attorney doing in the shower?'
jokes.setup: Barry calls Michael. He's at the prison but 'not making friends.' He explains the guards hate him for being a defense attorney there.
jokes.punchline: 'And the inmates hate me, 'cause what is a defense attorney doing in the shower?' — it's only in the shower that the inmates object to his presence.
jokes.explanation: The joke subverts expectation: the guards object to Barry being there professionally, but the INMATES object specifically to the shower location. The implication about why someone shouldn't be in a prison shower is left to the audience. Barry's total obliviousness ('Legally, it's the only place they can't record you') makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Legally, it's the only place they can't record you when you talk to inmates.' / Michael: 'I don't think that's true.' / Barry: 'Really?' / Michael: 'I got the worst clients.'
jokes.setup: Barry claims the shower is legally the only unrecorded location in a prison.
jokes.punchline: Michael contradicts him ('I don't think that's true'). Barry says 'Really?' — the genuine surprise of a lawyer who doesn't know basic legal facts. Michael: 'I got the worst clients.'
jokes.explanation: Barry's legal incompetence is a long-running joke. 'Really?' — the genuine shock of a lawyer learning his legal reasoning might be wrong — is a perfect character beat. 'I got the worst clients' from Michael is a great button (the Bluths thinking they have bad representation rather than that they're bad clients).

jokes.text: Barry: 'I prefer things face-to-face.' / Michael (aside): 'Less chance of a misunderstanding.'
jokes.setup: Barry insists on meeting in person — 'I prefer things face-to-face.'
jokes.punchline: Michael agrees for the stated reason 'less chance of a misunderstanding' — which immediately causes a massive misunderstanding (Michael bails out Barry instead of Buster).
jokes.explanation: The irony that 'less chance of a misunderstanding' leads directly to the biggest misunderstanding of the episode is well-structured dramatic irony. The joke pays off in the next scene.

jokes.text: The family gathers under a banner 'they'd been repurposing' for Buster's party — 'customized to Buster's limited palate.' Buster orders: 'I guess I'll take a hamburger with two tops.'
jokes.setup: The family has a welcome-home party with food customized for Buster.
jokes.punchline: 'I'll take a hamburger with two tops' — Buster orders a burger specifically with two top buns, no bottom, which is both a visual absurdity and a Buster character detail (infantilized food preferences).
jokes.explanation: 'Hamburger with two tops' is a small absurdist gem that tells you everything about Buster's infantilized psychology. He wants the soft, friendly part of the bun and none of the sturdy bottom. Character comedy at its most economical.

jokes.text: 'I guess I'll take a hamburger with two tops.'
jokes.setup: Buster is ordering food at his own welcome-home party
jokes.punchline: 'A hamburger with two tops' — ordering a burger without a bottom bun, because Buster inexplicably wants two top buns
jokes.explanation: 'A hamburger with two tops' is a wonderfully specific Buster order. He doesn't want a normal burger — he wants two top buns. It's a visual joke (imagine the food) combined with character comedy (Buster's infantile food preferences). Very economical — four words do all the work.

jokes.text: 'Maybe Buster made him stop to make sure nobody found the body.' / 'Lucille 2's fine. Believe me, she's somewhere with a new chin, sipping steak through a straw.'
jokes.setup: Barry is late bringing Buster. Someone jokes about Buster making Barry stop.
jokes.punchline: Lucille dismisses fears about Lucille 2 being dead: 'she's somewhere with a new chin, sipping steak through a straw.' A specific, absurd, and darkly comic image.
jokes.explanation: 'Sipping steak through a straw' after a chin job is a satisfying absurd detail — Lucille's casual dismissal of a potential murder victim in favor of a catty fashion prediction.

jokes.text: George Sr. suggests moving into the cottage with Tobias and Lucille because 'if Buster was a murderer, your mother would be in danger. Maybe I should move into the cottage with you two. That way, one of us can always be awake.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. voices concern about Buster being a murderer threatening Lucille.
jokes.punchline: The solution is moving in with Tobias and Lucille, taking turns being awake — treating a potential murderer son as a security-rotation problem.
jokes.explanation: The watch-rotation plan for a murderous son is dark but doesn't land with the precision of the episode's best moments. More setup than punchline.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'As your therapist, I encourage you to let go of relationships that don't serve you.' / Lucille: 'You're no longer my therapist.' / 'Okay. We're making progress.' / 'Then, as your roommate, I encourage you to claim your space.' / 'You're no longer my roommate.' / 'You are just progressing by leaps and bounds.'
jokes.setup: Tobias tries to offer advice to Lucille first as her therapist, then as her roommate.
jokes.punchline: Each title is revoked ('You're no longer my therapist.' 'You're no longer my roommate.') and Tobias responds to each rejection as a therapeutic win: 'We're making progress.' / 'You are just progressing by leaps and bounds.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best scenes. Tobias reframing every rejection as progress is a perfect encapsulation of his character — his therapist training means he hears 'you're not my therapist' as therapeutic growth. The escalating structure (therapist → roommate → 'A victim of my own competence') is beautifully constructed. 'A victim of my own competence' as his conclusion is an extraordinary line. Justifies 8.7.

jokes.text: 'You're no longer my roommate.' Tobias: 'You are just progressing by leaps and bounds.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has just fired Tobias as his therapist; Tobias called that 'progress'
jokes.punchline: George Sr. then fires him as roommate; Tobias doubles down: 'You are just progressing by leaps and bounds'
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the previous joke — Tobias reframes a second firing as additional progress. 'Leaps and bounds' when George Sr. has fired him twice in fifteen seconds is perfect. The running gag structure gives the joke added momentum: we now expect a third firing, which makes the 'A victim of my own competence' button even funnier.

jokes.text: 'A victim of my own competence.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has been rejected from both his therapist role and his roommate role by Lucille.
jokes.punchline: 'A victim of my own competence' — Tobias interprets his firing from both roles as evidence that he did his job so well he made himself redundant.
jokes.explanation: The single best line in the episode. 'A victim of my own competence' is a spectacular expression of Tobias's fundamental delusion — he is catastrophically incompetent at everything, and he experiences his incompetence as success. The phrase is lexically perfect: 'victim' implies he's suffering, 'competence' claims a skill he demonstrably doesn't have, and the combination reveals total absence of self-awareness. Approaches the calibration anchors' quality (comparable to 'Snip, snap!' level of character revelation). Justifies 9.4.

jokes.text: Tobias reveals Murphybrown as his 'bastard son' — 'They think we're boyfriends, Dad.' / 'Well, thanks for giving away that surprise! I'm his bastard father.'
jokes.setup: The family assumes Tobias and Murphybrown are boyfriends. Murphybrown corrects them.
jokes.punchline: Tobias confirms with a dramatic flourish: 'I'm his bastard father' — but the reveal is that he considers 'bastard father' a grander title than it is.
jokes.explanation: Tobias treating 'bastard father' as a proud announcement is a solid character moment. The boyfriend assumption is a light gag on Tobias's long-running sexuality ambiguity subtext.

jokes.text: 'His last name is... uh... Well, now it's Fünke.' / 'Oh, that's how you say it.' / 'Yes.' / 'He's named after a famous private investigator who worked for the Chicago Sun-Times... I believe it is a character from TV that was played by Candice Bergen.'
jokes.setup: Tobias explains that his son is named 'Murphybrown' (one word). George Sr. assumes he's named after Murphy Brown, the fictional TV reporter.
jokes.punchline: 'I believe it is a character from TV that was played by Candice Bergen. Mm. Well, that is a coincidence.' — Tobias denies the connection while describing it exactly, and the family accepts this. The name Murphybrown Fünke is itself absurd.
jokes.explanation: The joke is Tobias desperately denying the Murphy Brown reference while perfectly describing Murphy Brown. 'Well, that is a coincidence' delivered to a room full of people who obviously aren't convinced is solid absurdist character comedy.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Now I have two things to be embarrassed about, the second being keeping this a secret for so long, and I feel like I should apologize to maybe... half the people in this room.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is making an emotional speech about introducing his son to the family.
jokes.punchline: 'I feel like I should apologize to maybe... half the people in this room' — a non-committal emotional apology that specifically exempts half the room from consideration.
jokes.explanation: 'Maybe half the people' is a nicely Tobias qualifier — he's performing sincerity while limiting its scope. The pause before 'half' is doing the comedic work.

jokes.text: Tobias to Lucille: 'Then why am I wearing this?' — still in some form of costume after all his plans have changed
jokes.setup: Tobias has just learned Lindsay left; his elaborate plan to infiltrate the family via Lindsay's presence is moot
jokes.punchline: 'Then why am I wearing this?' — Tobias in an unexplained costume that now serves no purpose
jokes.explanation: The visual of Tobias in some unexplained costume with no purpose is pure Tobias. The line works best if you can see what he's wearing, making it a visual-dependent joke.

jokes.text: 'Oh, before I forget, this is your half-sister, Maeby.' / Murphybrown's reaction beat — 'Close one! I think she's rather very hot.'
jokes.setup: Tobias introduces Maeby to Murphybrown at the last possible moment before he would have pursued her.
jokes.punchline: 'Close one! I think she's rather very hot.' — Murphybrown reveals he was already attracted to Maeby, echoing the George Michael/Maeby dynamic and extending the show's cousin-attraction theme.
jokes.explanation: The show's long-running cousin-attraction joke gets a new generation twist: Murphybrown immediately finds Maeby attractive, extending the pattern. 'Close one!' acknowledges the near-miss. 'She's rather very hot' is an awkward construction that suits the character. The dark implication plays as both callback and escalation.

jokes.text: Barry arrives at the party: 'You guys, you are the best! This is so wonderful!' Everyone expects Buster. 'Barry? Where's Buster?' / 'In jail.' / 'Is this your cover just to throw me this party?'
jokes.setup: The family has gathered to welcome Buster home. Barry walks in first, expecting a party (because Michael paid his bail).
jokes.punchline: 'In jail.' — Buster is still in jail. The party is accidentally for Barry. Barry assumes it was planned.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the phone call misunderstanding. Barry walking into a welcome-home party for Buster and assuming it's for him — 'Is this your cover just to throw me this party?' — is both funny as a standalone and as a culmination of the entire Barry-in-jail subplot. 'In jail' as Michael's flat response is perfect. Justifies 8.0.

jokes.text: 'So, you're saying I paid to bail you out?' / 'You weren't gonna leave me there, were you?' / 'I did not know you were in jail.' / 'That's why I like to do things face-to-face, okay? Understood.'
jokes.setup: Michael realizes he paid Barry's bail instead of Buster's.
jokes.punchline: Barry cites the face-to-face preference ('That's why I like to do things face-to-face') as vindication — the very advice that caused the misunderstanding is now the lesson being drawn from the misunderstanding.
jokes.explanation: The circular logic — Barry's face-to-face rule caused the misunderstanding AND is now being cited as the lesson — is beautifully constructed. Barry's 'You weren't gonna leave me there, were you?' is also a great line: the lawyer's client is asking the client if he should have been abandoned in prison. The callback structure rewards the setup. Justifies 8.3.

jokes.text: The family laughs at Michael for accidentally bailing out Barry. 'This is so hysterical. This is priceless. One for the ages, Dad. No, this is never gonna get out of my system.'
jokes.setup: The family discovers Michael's mistake.
jokes.punchline: The Bluth family — who collectively have done far worse things — laughing at Michael's minor mistake with absolute delight. 'One for the ages, Dad' from G.O.B. is particularly absurd given his track record.
jokes.explanation: The family's disproportionate glee at Michael's mistake is a reliable character beat. Functional cringe comedy — the Bluths as schadenfreude merchants — but not particularly sharp on its own.

jokes.text: 'Geez, I hope Buster doesn't kill us for laughing.'
jokes.setup: The family is laughing at Michael's mistake. Buster is suspected of murder.
jokes.punchline: G.O.B.'s concern that their laughter might provoke Buster to kill them — a darkly casual acknowledgment that their brother might be a murderer.
jokes.explanation: The throwaway nature of the murder acknowledgment is the joke — G.O.B. treats potential fratricide as a minor etiquette concern. Economy is good.

jokes.text: 'I've never heard a man ask that question.' / 'Nor have I.'
jokes.setup: Murphybrown asks 'Am I the boy?' — a fundamentally childlike question from an adult.
jokes.punchline: Both Michael and Lucille confirm they've never heard a grown man ask if he is 'the boy.' Their synchronization implies it's the most unusual thing they've ever witnessed.
jokes.explanation: The synchronized disbelief of Michael and Lucille is a nice reaction beat. Two characters who have seen everything being genuinely stumped by this one question is the joke.

jokes.text: 'You know what would be a good idea? Why don't you get me a Fanta on your way out of the family. It's in the fridge.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is dismissing someone from the family conversation.
jokes.punchline: 'Get me a Fanta on your way out of the family' — Lucille instructs someone being excommunicated from the family to fetch her a drink on their way out, as if being ejected from the family is a trivial errand. Fanta is a callback to Buster and the Chanel man from the prison scene.
jokes.explanation: An extraordinary closing line. 'Get me a Fanta on your way out of the family' is quintessential Lucille — family ejection treated as a minor inconvenience that can serve her needs. The Fanta callback to Buster's childhood trauma (the Chanel man lost his job for giving Buster a Fanta) makes the choice of soft drink specifically cruel and layered. The 'It's in the fridge' as a practical afterthought perfectly deflates the drama of being thrown out of a family. Approaches the best lines in the series. Justifies 8.9.

jokes.text: 'I don't know what's happening.'
jokes.setup: Murphybrown has just been navigating a room of Bluths who are alternately welcoming and ejecting him.
jokes.punchline: 'I don't know what's happening.' — His simple, honest confusion is the closing beat of the episode.
jokes.explanation: A closing tag that works as both character comedy (Murphybrown's bewilderment) and meta-commentary (the audience may feel the same way). Simple, true, and appropriately deflating as a final note.

jokes.text: Final line: 'I don't know what's happening.' — Murphybrown's coda after the Fanta dismissal
jokes.setup: George Sr. has told Murphybrown to 'get a Fanta on your way out of the family'; the family closes ranks
jokes.punchline: 'I don't know what's happening' — a new character's bewildered response to being on the outside of a deeply layered family joke
jokes.explanation: A good button for the episode — Murphybrown's 'I don't know what's happening' is the audience surrogate response to the dense Fanta callback. It also characterizes him as an outsider to the family's dark logic, which is the correct response. Economical and well-placed as a closing beat.

tmdb_overview: The Bluths receive/present their award, but it weighs on George Sr. and creates new problems. Michael and George-Michael have a deep conversation.
insight: Subtle irony and character comedy sustain the episode despite below-elite scoring.
